serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried fish with ginger and scallion is approximately 150 g, representing one portion of fish with aromatics as served in a Singaporean home meal or hawker setting.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Fish with Ginger and Scallion — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo), Record F03020031
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record F03020031 for Stir Fried Fish with Ginger and Scallion. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked white fish with added oil (NDB No. 15264, Fish, mixed species, cooked, dry heat) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry; estimation basis documented in notes.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: Stir-fried fish with ginger and scallion contains only 2.95 g carbohydrate per 100 g, which is negligible. Per the Veritas negligible-carbohydrate rule, glycemic_index is set to null and gi_category to 'not_applicable'. The peer-reviewed GI reference (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) is cited as the authoritative GI framework source confirming that high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate fish dishes do not elicit a meaningful glycaemic response and are not assigned a GI value. Glycemic load per 150 g serving is effectively 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 334 mg per 100 g; borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 334 mg/100 g is below the 400 mg/100 g high-concern threshold but is borderline; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce during preparation, and limit portion size to keep total sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg/day target.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (2.95 g/100 g) and minimal sugar (0.43 g/100 g); high protein from fish supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 2.95 g carbohydrate and 0.43 g sugar per 100 g, this dish has a negligible glycaemic impact; the high protein content (7.96 g/100 g) from fish slows gastric emptying and supports postprandial blood glucose control, making it a suitable choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean fish protein with moderate total fat (7.16 g/100 g) and low estimated saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g); ginger provides anti-inflammatory gingerols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This dish provides lean fish protein with low saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g) and anti-inflammatory ginger compounds (gingerols); if fatty fish such as mackerel or salmon is used, the omega-3 fatty acid content further supports cardiovascular health, though sodium at 334 mg/100 g should be moderated for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ginger provides anti-inflammatory gingerols; sodium at 334 mg/100 g may contribute to tissue swelling; omega-3 benefit depends on fish species used
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger's gingerols offer evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; however, sodium at 334 mg/100 g may contribute to fluid retention and joint swelling, so reduced seasoning is recommended, and selecting fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) maximises omega-3 anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Fish with Ginger and Scallion (姜葱炒鱼; Jiang cong chao yu) is a classic Singaporean Chinese home-style and hawker dish featuring pieces of fish — most commonly pomfret (白鲳, bai chang) — stir-fried at high heat with fresh ginger slices, scallions (spring onions), shallots, and garlic. The fish is typically marinated briefly in soy sauce, a small amount of cornstarch, and sesame oil before being wok-fried in vegetable oil. Oyster sauce and light soy sauce are added during cooking to create a savoury, slightly caramelised glaze. The texture is tender and moist with a lightly charred exterior from wok hei (breath of the wok). The flavour profile is savoury-umami with the warmth of ginger and the mild sweetness of scallion. Nutritionally, this dish provides 108 kcal per 100 g with 7.96 g protein, 7.16 g fat, and only 2.95 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate option. Sodium is 334 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertension management. The dish is suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible glycaemic impact, and for cardiovascular health when prepared with fatty fish. Ginger's gingerols provide anti-inflammatory properties relevant to joint health. This dish is a staple in Singaporean Chinese family meals and is commonly served with steamed white rice as part of a multi-dish spread.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F03020031), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The food identity, description, and cultural context are well-established. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy analogue data and is clearly documented. GI source is a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). All other fields are grounded in approved sources or defensibly estimated.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for cooked white fish with added oil (NDB No. 15264); HPB does not report fatty acid fractions for this entry. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture data for this specific prepared dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. Glycemic index is not applicable as this dish contains negligible carbohydrates (2.95 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per the Veritas negligible-carbohydrate GI rule. GI source is Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), a peer-reviewed journal, confirming that very-low-carbohydrate fish dishes are not assigned a GI value. 4. The dish description specifies pomfret as the primary fish; however, the generic record may apply to other white fish species. Nutritional values may vary if fatty fish (salmon, mackerel) is substituted. 5. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation with fish and plant-based aromatics; oyster sauce may contain shellfish derivatives — consumers should verify halal certification of condiments used. 7.
